3:
"And when the people were come into the camp, the elders of Israel said, Wherefore hath the LORD smitten us to day before the Philistines? Let us fetch the ark of the covenant of the LORD out of Shiloh unto us, that, when it cometh among us, it may save us out of the hand of our enemies." The witnesses note Israel's counsel to fetch the ark that it might save them — the crux of the superstition that trusts in the outward symbol of God's presence rather than in God himself. On "Let us fetch the ark... that, when it cometh among us, it may save us," interpreters expound the counsel. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the trust in the symbol rather than God — "the elders of Israel said, Wherefore hath the LORD smitten us... Let us fetch the ark of the covenant of the LORD out of Shiloh unto us, that, when it cometh among us, it may save us": Israel, defeated, resolves to bring the ark into the camp as a charm to win the battle; the symbol trusted in place of God; the superstition that makes a charm of the holy); the truth that God is not bound to his symbols, and no outward thing can save where there is no repentance and no true faith, "this people draweth nigh unto me with their mouth... but their heart is far from me," Matthew 15:8; The figure of the superstition that trusts in the outward symbol of God's presence rather than in God himself.

21:
"And she named the child Ichabod, saying, The glory is departed from Israel: because the ark of God was taken, and because of her father in law and her husband." The witnesses note the dying woman naming her child Ichabod, "the glory is departed," because the ark of God was taken — the crux of the departed glory, that the true glory of God's people is his presence, and that its loss is the heaviest of calamities. On "she named the child Ichabod, saying, The glory is departed from Israel: because the ark of God was taken," interpreters expound the name. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the glory that is God's presence — "and she named the child Ichabod, saying, The glory is departed from Israel: because the ark of God was taken": the dying woman sets on her son the name Ichabod, "no glory" or "where is the glory," because the ark, the sign of God's presence, was lost; the truth that the worst calamity that can befall a church or a soul is the departure of God's presence, the Ichabod written over a people from whom God has withdrawn, "woe also to them when I depart from them," Hosea 9:12;. The figure of the departed glory, that the true glory of God's people is his presence, and that its loss is the heaviest of calamities.

On "because the ark of God was taken, and because of her father in law and her husband," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the ark as the glory. The verse displays the glory departed because the ark was taken.

The witnesses agree the substance: the dying woman sets on her son the name Ichabod because the ark, the sign of God's presence, was lost (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the departed glory: The glory is departed from Israel: because the ark of God was taken — the profound truth that the glory of Israel was not her armies or wealth but the presence of God symbolized by the ark, so that with the ark taken the glory was departed, her grief for the ark outweighing even the loss of husband and father, the name a perpetual memorial of the calamity, the true glory and strength of God's people the presence of God among them (Psalm 127:1), the glory that is the presence of God fulfilled in the Word made flesh (John 1:14). The verse displays the departed-glory crux. The glory departed because the ark was taken displays the crux: she named the child Ichabod, saying, The glory is departed from Israel: because the ark of God was taken (Psalm 127:1; Hosea 9:12; John 1:14) — the departed glory, that the true glory of God's people is his presence, and its loss the heaviest of calamities. The verse displays the woman's last word, The glory is departed, for the ark is taken (vv.22).
22:
"And she said, The glory is departed from Israel: for the ark of God is taken." The very brief verse notes the dying woman's last word, that the glory was departed for the ark was taken. The grammatical voices note the repetition. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "she said, The glory is departed from Israel: for the ark of God is taken" — with her dying breath she repeated that the glory was gone; the and she said, The glory is departed from Israel (the glory departed); the for the ark of God is taken (because the ark is taken); the figure (the dying repetition that fixed the cause of the calamity, the ark the glory; the truth that she repeated the glory was gone because the ark was taken, 1 Samuel 4:21; the figure of the heart that lays the loss of God's presence to heart above all; the last word; the ark the glory, vv.21). The verse closes the chapter of the ark taken. The verse displays the dying woman's solemn repetition, fixing the heart of the calamity not in the deaths of her kindred but in the loss of the ark and the presence of God. The verse displays the truth that the departure of God's presence is the true and deepest calamity of his people, to be laid to heart above every other loss.

On "And she said, The glory is departed from Israel," the witnesses note the glory. Gill notes the dying woman repeating that the glory was gone (the lament, 1 Samuel 4:21) — the glory (departed from Israel).

On "for the ark of God is taken," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the cause: "for the ark of God is taken" — the ark taken the cause of the departed glory (the loss); the figure (the heart that lays the loss of God's presence to heart above all); the providence (the last word, the ark in the land of the Philistines to follow, ch.5). The verse displays the dying woman's last word on the departed glory.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the repetition — "The glory is departed from Israel: for the ark of God is taken," the repetition (Keil, Gill); "And she said, The glory is departed from Israel," the glory (1 Samuel 4:21, Gill); "for the ark of God is taken," the cause (Ellicott, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the repetition. The verse marks the woman: dying. The verse displays the glory: departed. The verse displays the ark: taken. The verse displays the cause: fixed. The repetition displays the heart that lays the loss of God's presence to heart above all. The verse displays the lament: repeated. The verse displays the loss: laid to heart. The verse displays the presence: gone. The dying woman's last word on the departed glory displays the heart that lays the loss of God's presence to heart above all: The glory is departed from Israel: for the ark of God is taken (1 Samuel 4:21) — with her dying breath repeating that the glory was gone because the ark was taken, the heart that lays the loss of God's presence to heart above all. The verse closes the chapter, before the ark among the Philistines (ch.5).
